preFix.title: Cutting and fitting new stair treads
preFix.subtitle: Replace worn or damaged timber stair treads on a closed-string staircase
preFix.excerpt: How to remove old stair treads and fit replacements on a domestic closed-string staircase, covering releasing the tread from its housings, measuring the replacement, scribing to the wall string, and securing the new tread with glue and screws.
preFix.sourceNotes: Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry and Joinery. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics (stair construction). Collins Complete DIY Manual. Building Regulations Approved Document K (stairs).

content.text: On a
closed-string staircase
, each tread is held in
housing joints

cut into each string, with a wedge driven in from below to lock it. Releasing the old tread requires breaking out the wedge and the old glue from below. The new tread is cut to match, scribed to the wall string, and glued and screwed into position.
What you will need: materials

New stair tread timber: typically 38 mm thick softwood (pine or hemlock) or hardwood (oak, ash) to match the existing. The tread must be wide enough to cover the housing groove plus the nosing overhang, typically 225 to 250 mm. Cut to the length of the existing tread plus 20 mm for scribing. A packet of glued timber wedges for re-securing the tread in the housing, or construction adhesive. No. 8 x 75 mm screws for any additional face fixings.
Step 1, remove the old tread

Access the underside of the stair from below (through a cupboard door under the stairs, or by removing the stair soffit lining). Identify the wedges that lock the tread in its housing: they are driven in from below the tread into the groove in the string. Use the chisel and mallet to split or knock the wedges back toward the middle of the tread. Once the wedge is free, the tread can be tapped up from below and lifted out. Old glue in the housing will resist: work along the length, tapping from below, until it releases fully. Clear the old glue and splinters from the housing with the chisel before fitting the new tread.
Step 2, measure and cut the new tread

Measure the distance between the housings in both strings at the front and back of the tread position. The two measurements may differ if the strings are not parallel (common in older houses). Cut the new tread 2 mm shorter than the tighter of the two measurements: it must slide into the housings without binding. The
stair nosing
should project 20 to 25 mm forward of the riser face below. Round or bullnose the front edge of the nosing with a block plane or sandpaper to match the existing treads.
Step 3, scribe the tread to the wall string

The wall end of the tread must follow the profile of the wall string's housing and any irregularity in the wall surface. Hold the tread in position and scribe the wall-string end with a compass set to the largest gap between tread and string. Cut along the scribed line with the coping saw and plane back to the line. Slide the tread into its housing: it should fit without gaps at the wall end.
Step 4, glue and fix the tread

Apply PVA to the housing grooves and to the wedge faces. Slide the tread into both housings simultaneously. Drive the wedges back in from below to lock the tread tight in the housing. Apply downward pressure on the tread surface while the wedges are driven. Drill and countersink two No. 8 x 75 mm screws through the top face of the tread into the riser below at the front edge, 50 mm from each end. Fill the screw heads and sand flush.
Variations

For an open-string staircase where the tread ends are visible, the replacement tread may need mitred returns at the open end to match the existing profile. Open-string treads are screwed and glued from the front rather than wedged from below. The process of removal is the same (splitting the glue bond from below) but the replacement fitting is from above.
Care

New softwood treads are bare timber and need sealing before use: apply two coats of floor varnish or hard-wearing floor paint. Wax or oil finishes wear faster on stairs than on floors. Check the tread fixings annually and re-drive any screws that have worked loose: a loose tread on a stair is a trip hazard.

attrs.fix: Re-drive the wedge from below with additional PVA on the wedge face. If the squeak is at the front edge, apply a bead of PVA between the tread front and the riser face.
attrs.cause: The wedge has not fully locked the tread in the housing; or the tread is rubbing against the riser above.
attrs.symptom: New tread squeaks underfoot after fitting

attrs.fix: Remove the tread, clean the housing completely with the chisel, and refit. The housing depth is the controlling dimension: any material left in it raises the tread above the correct level.
attrs.cause: Old glue or debris left in the housing is preventing the tread from seating fully.
attrs.symptom: Tread sits 3 to 5 mm higher than adjacent treads after fitting

attrs.fix: Fill the gap with decorator's caulk or an appropriate timber fillet pinned in place. The string housing covers most of the gap; only the outer face needs attention.
attrs.cause: Scribing step was skipped; the wall is not plumb or the string is not straight.
attrs.symptom: Gap at the wall end between tread and wall string
